facts.t: The Centennial Exposition opened in Philadelphia, the first official World's Fair held in the United States.
Alexander Graham Bell was granted a patent for the telephone just days before completing the first call.
Custer and over 200 US cavalrymen were wiped out by Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho warriors at Little Bighorn.
Anheuser-Busch introduced Budweiser, a lager named for the Bohemian brewing town of Budweis.
Baseball's National League, still the oldest surviving pro sports league in America, was founded.
The Compromise of 1877 ended Reconstruction and installed Rutherford B. Hayes in the White House.
Thomas Edison unveiled the phonograph, the first machine able to record and play back sound.
Federal troops crushed the Great Railroad Strike, the first major nationwide labor uprising in US history.
Chief Joseph surrendered after the Nez Perce War, famously vowing to "fight no more forever."
Astronomer Asaph Hall discovered Phobos and Deimos, the two moons of Mars.
A yellow fever epidemic tore through the Mississippi Valley, killing thousands in Memphis alone.
New Haven, Connecticut opened the first commercial telephone exchange in the United States.
Emma Nutt became the world's first female telephone switchboard operator, hired in Boston.
Congress passed the Bland-Allison Act over President Hayes's veto, forcing the Treasury to buy silver.
Joseph Pulitzer bought the St. Louis Post-Dispatch, launching his newspaper empire.
Thomas Edison demonstrated a practical incandescent light bulb at his Menlo Park lab.
F.W. Woolworth opened his first successful five-and-dime store, in Lancaster, Pennsylvania.
Thousands of Black Southerners, the "Exodusters," migrated to Kansas seeking land and freedom.
Inventor George Selden filed the first US patent application for a gasoline-powered automobile.
Western Union and Bell Telephone settled their patent war, leaving Bell in control of US phone service.
James Garfield was elected the 20th president of the United States.
Wabash, Indiana became the first city in the world lit entirely by electric arc lights.
The Salvation Army began operating in the United States, launching its work in Philadelphia.
The US census counted just over 50 million Americans for the first time.
Thomas Edison received the patent for the incandescent light bulb design he'd perfected.
President James Garfield was shot by a disgruntled office-seeker and died months later.
Wyatt Earp, Doc Holliday, and the Clantons shot it out at the O.K. Corral in Tombstone, Arizona.
Clara Barton founded the American Red Cross to bring disaster relief to the United States.
Booker T. Washington opened the Tuskegee Institute to educate formerly enslaved Americans.
Outlaw Billy the Kid was shot and killed by Sheriff Pat Garrett in New Mexico Territory.
Congress passed the Chinese Exclusion Act, the first US law to ban immigration by nationality.
Jesse James was shot and killed by gang member Robert Ford for a bounty and a pardon.
John D. Rockefeller consolidated his holdings into the Standard Oil Trust.
New York City held the first Labor Day parade, marching 10,000 workers up Broadway.
Edison's Pearl Street Station lit up Lower Manhattan, the first commercial power plant in the US.
The Brooklyn Bridge opened, then the longest suspension bridge in the world.
Railroads adopted standard time zones across the US and Canada on the "Day of Two Noons."
Buffalo Bill's Wild West show debuted in North Platte, Nebraska, launching a national phenomenon.
The Pendleton Act created a merit-based civil service, ending the worst of the old patronage system.
The Metropolitan Opera House opened its doors in New York City.
The Washington Monument was completed, briefly making it the tallest structure on Earth.
Grover Cleveland was elected president, the first Democrat to win the White House since the Civil War.
Lewis Waterman patented the first practical fountain pen that wouldn't leak or blot.
Coney Island opened the Switchback Railway, considered America's first great roller coaster.
Charles Dow published his first stock average, the ancestor of the Dow Jones Industrial Average.
The Statue of Liberty's crated pieces arrived in New York Harbor aboard the French ship Isere.
Chicago's Home Insurance Building was completed, often called the world's first skyscraper.
Grover Cleveland was inaugurated as the 22nd president of the United States.
The Washington Monument was formally dedicated in a ceremony attended by President Arthur.
Atlanta pharmacist John Pemberton began selling "French Wine Coca," a forerunner of Coca-Cola.
The Statue of Liberty was dedicated in New York Harbor before a crowd of thousands.
John Pemberton invented Coca-Cola in Atlanta after local prohibition forced him to drop the wine from his tonic.
A bombing at Chicago's Haymarket Square during a labor rally killed police and bystanders alike.
Samuel Gompers helped found the American Federation of Labor to organize skilled workers nationally.
Apache leader Geronimo surrendered to the US Army, ending decades of armed resistance.
The Interstate Commerce Act created the ICC, the first federal agency to regulate American business.
The Dawes Act broke up tribal lands into individual allotments, stripping millions of acres from Native nations.
Anne Sullivan began teaching young Helen Keller, breaking through her isolation within weeks.
Punxsutawney, Pennsylvania held its first recorded Groundhog Day, a tradition that stuck.
Buffalo Bill's Wild West show toured Britain, performing for Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee.
A ferocious March blizzard buried the Northeast, killing hundreds and paralyzing New York City.
The "Schoolhouse Blizzard" struck the Great Plains without warning, killing dozens of children walking home.
George Eastman introduced the Kodak camera, promising "you press the button, we do the rest."
A group of explorers and scientists founded the National Geographic Society in Washington, DC.
The Washington Monument finally opened to the public after years of construction delays.
Tens of thousands of settlers raced to claim land in the Oklahoma Land Run's opening day.
North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, and Washington all joined the Union within weeks of each other.
A dam failure unleashed the Johnstown Flood on Pennsylvania, killing over 2,200 people.
Charles Dow and two partners launched The Wall Street Journal.
A San Francisco saloon installed the first coin-operated jukebox, a nickel-a-song phonograph.
US troops killed roughly 250 Lakota men, women, and children at Wounded Knee, South Dakota.
The Sherman Antitrust Act became the first federal law aimed squarely at monopolies.
Wyoming joined the Union as the first state to grant women full voting rights.
Jacob Riis's How the Other Half Lives exposed the squalor of New York's tenements to the nation.
The Census Bureau declared the American frontier officially closed for the first time.
James Naismith nailed up two peach baskets in Springfield, Massachusetts, and invented basketball.
The Forest Reserve Act let presidents set aside public land as national forests.
The Chace Act extended US copyright protection to foreign authors for the first time.
Thomas Edison patented the Kinetograph, an early motion-picture camera.
William Wrigley Jr. founded his chewing gum company in Chicago.
Ellis Island opened in New York Harbor, becoming the nation's busiest immigration gateway.
American schoolchildren first recited Francis Bellamy's new Pledge of Allegiance.
The violent Homestead Strike erupted at Carnegie's steel plant in Pennsylvania.
John Muir founded the Sierra Club to protect America's wild places.
Asa Candler formally incorporated The Coca-Cola Company in Georgia.
The World's Columbian Exposition opened in Chicago, celebrating 400 years since Columbus.
The Panic of 1893 triggered one of the worst economic depressions in US history.
George Ferris unveiled the original Ferris Wheel at the Chicago World's Fair.
Cracker Jack, the molasses-coated popcorn and peanut snack, debuted at the Chicago World's Fair.
A view from Pikes Peak inspired Katharine Lee Bates to write "America the Beautiful."
The nationwide Pullman railroad strike paralyzed American rail traffic for weeks.
Congress made Labor Day a federal holiday, partly to calm tensions after the Pullman Strike.
Coxey's Army, a ragtag march of unemployed workers, arrived in Washington demanding jobs.
New York City's first Kinetoscope parlor let paying customers watch Edison's short films.
Milton Hershey began manufacturing chocolate, planting the seed of the Hershey Company.
Frank Duryea won America's first automobile race, the Chicago Times-Herald race, on Thanksgiving.
The Supreme Court struck down the federal income tax as unconstitutional in Pollock v. Farmers' Loan.
George Selden finally received his automobile patent, 16 years after he first filed it.
The national Anti-Saloon League formed in Washington, DC, launching the campaign for Prohibition.
The New York Public Library was founded, merging several private collections into one institution.
The Supreme Court upheld racial segregation as constitutional in Plessy v. Ferguson.
Utah entered the Union as the 45th state after decades of seeking statehood.
American James Connolly won the triple jump in Athens, the first Olympic champion in 1,500 years.
The Duryea Motor Wagon Company became the first American firm to sell gasoline automobiles.
John Philip Sousa composed "The Stars and Stripes Forever" while sailing home for Christmas.
Boston opened the Tremont Street Subway, the first underground rail line in North America.
William McKinley was inaugurated as the 25th president of the United States.
The New York Sun ran its famous editorial assuring a child that Santa Claus is real.
Ransom Olds founded one of America's earliest automobile manufacturing companies.
Runners raced in the first Boston Marathon, the country's oldest annual marathon.
The battleship USS Maine exploded in Havana Harbor, helping spark the Spanish-American War.
The United States formally annexed the Republic of Hawaii.
Theodore Roosevelt and the Rough Riders charged up San Juan Hill in Cuba.
Pharmacist Caleb Bradham renamed his soda "Pepsi-Cola" in New Bern, North Carolina.
The Treaty of Paris ended the war, handing the US Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines.
The Philippine-American War began as Filipino forces resisted new US colonial rule.
Scott Joplin published "Maple Leaf Rag," helping ragtime music sweep the nation.
Secretary of State John Hay announced the Open Door Policy toward China.
New York cab driver Jacob German earned the first speeding ticket in US history, for going 12 mph.
Congress established Mount Rainier National Park in Washington, the country's fifth national park.
A hurricane devastated Galveston, Texas, still the deadliest natural disaster in US history.
The 1900 census counted roughly 76 million people living in the United States.
Hawaii was formally organized as a US territory under the Hawaiian Organic Act.
Railroad engineer Casey Jones died in a famous wreck that inspired a lasting folk ballad.
L. Frank Baum published "The Wonderful Wizard of Oz."
